does: This paper applies control-theoretic analysis to determine whether externally enforced safety strategies can maintain safety as AI systems become increasingly powerful. Using formal control-theoretic principles, it establishes two main results: first, a class-wide impossibility theorem proving that once a system's capabilities exceed what bounded external control can counteract, no safety strategy relying on continued external enforcement can sustainably prevent unsafe behavior. This is a structural failure across all externally enforced approaches rather than a flaw specific to any single strategy. Second, it derives a conditional necessity result characterizing what any viable alternative strategy must satisfy structurally if one exists at all after external approaches fail.
connects: This work extends the long-standing debate in AI safety about whether control and containment can reliably prevent advanced AI systems from causing harm. It responds to proposals for indefinite external oversight and builds on prior research questioning whether external constraints scale with capability growth. The paper connects to control-theoretic foundations used in formal verification and autonomous systems safety, adapting reachability and controllability concepts to the AI safety domain. It engages with emerging literature on the limits of alignment through external governance and the necessity of internal safety mechanisms, contributing to broader discussions about whether safe superintelligence requires internalized constraints rather than perpetual external supervision.
enables: This framework creates a foundation for analyzing when and why AI safety strategies must shift from external enforcement to internal mechanisms. By characterizing structural requirements for any viable alternative approach after external strategies fail, it guides research into what constitutes a genuinely sustainable safety architecture. The results suggest future work should focus on how to build or incentivize systems with intrinsic commitment to safety goals, formal verification of internal properties that guarantee compliance independent of external monitoring, and rigorous analysis of scalable robustness guarantees. It opens questions about whether certain safety properties must be embedded at design time or learned through training, and how to verify such internalization before deployment of transformatively powerful systems.
